skill.name: translational-gap-analyzer
skill.description: Assess translational gaps between preclinical models and human diseases.
skill.summary: Systematically evaluates physiological, genetic, and metabolic differences between animal models and humans to identify clinical translation risks.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: After evaluating all 65 static findings, 63 are false positives from pattern matching on Markdown documentation and text content. The 2 true positives are file write operations at scripts/main.py:642 and scripts/main.py:668 that write report output to user-specified paths without path traversal validation. This is standard CLI tool behavior with minimal risk in a sandboxed environment. No malicious intent, credential theft, data exfiltration, or command injection vectors were found. The skill is a legitimate medical research analysis tool.

low_findings.title: Unvalidated file write path
low_findings.description: Output file path from user argument is used directly in open() without directory traversal validation. An attacker controlling the --output argument could write files outside the intended workspace directory.

dangerous_patterns.title: File write without path validation
dangerous_patterns.description: The --output argument is passed directly to open() with write mode. No sanitization or directory scope check is performed on the output path. Risk is minimal in sandboxed environments but should be documented.

content.user_title: Analyze Translational Gaps in Preclinical Research
content.value_statement: Translational research often fails because preclinical models do not perfectly represent human diseases. This skill systematically evaluates physiological, genetic, and metabolic differences between animal models and humans to identify clinical translation risks early.
actual_capabilities: Evaluate anatomical and physiological differences between research models and humans
Analyze disease-specific translational risks for conditions including Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, cancer, and diabetes
Generate multi-model comparison reports to rank preclinical models by translational suitability
Produce structured gap analysis reports in JSON, Markdown, or table format
Identify clinical trial failure risk factors based on model-disease dimension scores
Provide targeted improvement recommendations to increase translation success rates
limitations: Cannot access external databases or live literature for real-time data
Analysis is based on static knowledge, not individual experimental results
Does not replace regulatory or ethics committee review
Limited to documented disease and model type combinations

use_cases.title: Preclinical Model Suitability Assessment
use_cases.description: Evaluate how well a specific animal model represents a human disease before starting experiments.
use_cases.target_user: Preclinical researcher selecting animal models for study design

use_cases.title: Multi-Model Comparison for Drug Development
use_cases.description: Compare multiple animal models side by side to select the optimal model for drug efficacy and safety testing.
use_cases.target_user: Drug discovery team evaluating model options for a new therapeutic

use_cases.title: Grant Proposal Translational Validity Review
use_cases.description: Assess the translational strength of proposed research methodology for funding applications.
use_cases.target_user: Grant reviewer or principal investigator evaluating research proposals

prompt_templates.title: Quick Single Model Risk Assessment
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a quick assessment of the [model_type] model for [disease_name]. Show overall gap score, risk level, and top concerns in table format.
prompt_templates.scenario: A researcher needs a fast overview of how well a mouse model works for studying Alzheimer's disease before planning experiments.

prompt_templates.title: Full Multi-Dimensional Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a full assessment of the [model_type] model for [disease_name] with focus on [anatomy, metabolism, immune]. Generate a detailed Markdown report with dimension scores, clinical failure predictors, and recommendations.
prompt_templates.scenario: A pharmaceutical team needs a comprehensive gap analysis of the primate model for Parkinson's disease focusing on immune and metabolic dimensions.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Model Comparison
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare the models [model_1, model_2, model_3] for [disease_name]. Show a ranked comparison table and highlight the best and worst performing models with their key limitations.
prompt_templates.scenario: A lab director is deciding between mouse, rat, and zebrafish models for cardiovascular research and needs a data-driven comparison.

prompt_templates.title: Custom Analysis with Specific Output Format
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the [model_type] model for [disease_name] focusing on [focus_areas]. Generate a JSON report with all dimension details and risk predictors. Save output to [output_path].
prompt_templates.scenario: An experienced bioinformatician needs a machine-readable JSON report of translational gaps for integration into a larger analysis pipeline.

output_examples.input: Assess the mouse model for Alzheimer's disease. Generate a full report with dimension scores.
output_examples.output: Overall gap score of 6.8 out of 10, risk level HIGH. The immune dimension scores 5.5 due to microglia functional differences. Genetics scores 8.5 due to APOE4 differences and different tau pathology patterns. Top recommendation: consider using humanized mouse models and add non-human primate validation.

output_examples.input: Compare mouse, rat, and primate models for Parkinson's disease. Show the best model.
output_examples.output: Primate ranks first with the lowest gap score of 3.8, followed by rat at 6.5 and mouse at 7.2. Mouse models lack natural Lewy bodies and have dopamine system differences. Primate models show high genetic similarity but come with higher cost and ethical considerations.

output_examples.input: Quick assessment of the zebrafish model for cancer research.
output_examples.output: Risk level CRITICAL with overall gap score of 8.2. Key concerns: adaptive immunity score of 9.0, drug metabolism score of 8.5. Recommendation: use mainly for high-throughput screening and validate positive results in mammalian models.

best_practices: Always specify both the model type and target disease before running analysis to get accurate dimension scores
Use multi-model comparison mode when the research design is not yet fixed to select the most translational relevant model
Review the focus areas parameter to align the analysis with specific research questions and resource constraints
anti_patterns: Using gap scores as the sole criterion for model selection without considering practical constraints like cost and ethical approval
Analyzing models without specifying the target disease, which prevents disease-specific risk factor evaluation
Assuming low gap scores guarantee clinical translation success without additional validation experiments

faq.question: What is a translational gap?
faq.answer: A translational gap is the difference between how well a preclinical model represents a human disease. A larger gap means the model is less likely to predict clinical outcomes accurately.

faq.question: Which animal models are supported?
faq.answer: The skill supports mouse, rat, zebrafish, cell line, organoid, and primate models. Each model has specific strengths and limitations for different research questions.

faq.question: What diseases can be analyzed?
faq.answer: The built-in knowledge covers Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, cancer, diabetes, autoimmune diseases, and cardiovascular disease. Other disease names are accepted but produce less specific results.

faq.question: How are gap scores calculated?
faq.answer: Scores range from 0 to 10 based on anatomical, physiological, metabolic, immune, genetic, and behavioral differences between the model and humans. Higher scores mean larger gaps.

faq.question: What output formats are available?
faq.answer: Results can be generated in JSON for machine processing, Markdown for documentation, or table format for quick review. Use the --format argument to choose.

faq.question: Can I compare multiple models in one run?
faq.answer: Yes. Use the --models argument with comma-separated model names and the --compare flag to generate a ranked comparison of multiple models for the same disease.
